channel.title: Manoj's Daily Essay Picks
channel.description: Five serious articles and essays selected daily across science, ideas, culture, personal growth, politics, business, strategy, and technology.

item.title: ‘Spermageddon’: is the world facing a male reproductive crisis?
item.description: A careful investigation of falling sperm counts, testosterone, metabolic health, pollution claims, and what the evidence does—and does not—show.

item.title: The U.S. wants to contain China’s AI. Silicon Valley keeps using it
item.description: Chinese open-weight models are becoming inputs to American AI development, revealing the limits of technology containment and the strategic power of ecosystems.

item.title: The Protests Gripping India — and Rattling Modi
item.description: How exam failures, youth precarity, satire, and state repression are turning a narrow grievance into a wider challenge to political legitimacy.

item.title: The savage bites back
item.description: How Brazilian modernists turned the colonial fantasy of cannibalism into a theory of cultural agency: absorb, transform, and return borrowed forms on your own terms.

item.title: Blindsighted
item.description: A critique of Neuralink’s vision-restoration ambitions and the ideology that turns disability, perception, and human augmentation into engineering abstractions.

item.title: The Organisms That Make Earth’s Harshest Places Home
item.description: A visual tour of extremophiles—and what their chemistry reveals about biotechnology, life’s origins, and possible life beyond Earth.

item.title: What Humanity Needs To Flourish In The Next Decade
item.description: A serious agenda for “reverse alignment”: redesigning institutions, laws, skills, and governance so society can absorb AI without sacrificing prosperity or accountability.

item.title: ‘She was almost a Batman figure’: Mexico’s avenging Mother Courage
item.description: How a grieving mother became a national anti-crime hero—and how coercion, weak evidence, media power, and institutional capture complicated the case she built.

item.title: Elite Young Runners Are Becoming Freakishly Fast. Welcome to ‘Trackflation’
item.description: Youth running’s performance boom reveals how coaching, technology, money, social media, and escalating expectations can raise achievement while increasing risk.

item.title: When the world stopped moving
item.description: An incarcerated writer describes learning to notice the small space between impulse and reaction without romanticizing the conditions that forced the lesson.

item.title: China’s AI talent race is starting in high school
item.description: Chinese technology companies are extending AI talent pipelines into secondary school, challenging conventional assumptions about credentials, training, and when firms should identify exceptional ability.

item.title: ‘Most people don’t know we’ve set up a doomsday system’: physicist Laura Grego on satellites, nuclear weapons and the battle for our skies
item.description: Physicist Laura Grego explains how military dependence, commercial satellite expansion, fragile treaties, and compressed nuclear decision times are turning space into critical and dangerously under-governed infrastructure.

item.title: How the War in Ukraine Reshaped Israel’s ‘Russian’ Community
item.description: A reported account of how war, migration, language, and generational change fractured Israel’s once-monolithic “Russian” identity into competing political communities.

item.title: What ancient art tells us about modern psychedelic medicine
item.description: Lower Pecos rock art becomes a prompt to ask whether psychedelic medicine loses therapeutic value when it isolates molecules from ritual, place, story, and community.

item.title: The Paths Forward for the Gulf States and Iran
item.description: Why Gulf governments are likely to manage rather than resolve their conflict with Iran, balancing deterrence, diplomacy, economic exposure, and dependence on outside security powers.

item.title: Transit of wonder
item.description: A history of the transit of Venus and the astronomers who crossed the world to turn a fleeting shadow into a ruler for the solar system.

item.title: Mesa-Optimization is Destroying Education
item.description: How Campbell’s Law and mesa-optimization explain schools that maximize grades and credentials while losing sight of actual learning.

item.title: Pakistan’s Rise To A Middle Power
item.description: Bina Shah argues that Pakistan’s brokerage among rival powers is elevating it into a more consequential middle-power role.

item.title: Martin Picard’s Mitochondrial Theory of Mind
item.description: A rigorous profile of the emerging idea that mitochondrial energy flow may connect cellular biology with health, mood, and conscious experience.

item.title: The Ableism of Kings, the Fury of Trump
item.description: A reading of Homer and contemporary politics that shows how leaders turn bodily difference into a weapon for discrediting rivals and dissenters.

item.title: The Problem AI Content Moderation Cannot Solve
item.description: Why automated moderation misses harms rooted not in explicit content, but in consent, context, reputation, and culturally specific forms of abuse.

item.title: The Heated Debate In Europe Over Air Conditioning
item.description: Europe’s discomfort with air conditioning reveals why climate mitigation and adaptation must be treated as complementary infrastructure strategies.

item.title: Parapsychology Is Coming in From the Cold
item.description: A reported history of Cambridge’s psychical-research tradition and the uneasy boundary between consciousness studies, fringe inquiry, and mainstream science.

item.title: What a Picture of a Dog Has to Do with Reason and Logic
item.description: Aphantasia challenges the old claim that abstract thought requires mental pictures, pointing instead toward a pluralist view of human cognition.

item.title: Susanna Clarke: ‘I had been ill for 11 years. I felt like I was about to fall off the world’
item.description: Susanna Clarke reflects on chronic illness, the limits of language, and the stories sufferers use to make uncertainty livable.

item.title: India’s crackdown on a new WhatsApp feature risks setting a global precedent
item.description: India’s objection to WhatsApp usernames shows how one large market can reshape privacy architecture and product standards worldwide.

item.title: Same art, different name: would you see or hear it the same way?
item.description: A misattributed concerto reveals how reputation changes attention, interpretation, and even the pleasure we experience from art.

item.title: It’s Not Easy Being Green
item.description: A searching account of music’s environmental footprint and why easy green fixes can conceal the industry’s deeper structures and tradeoffs.

item.title: Thermodynamic Computers Go With the (Energy) Flow
item.description: A lucid look at computers that use thermal noise as a resource rather than an error source—and why the idea could radically reduce energy use.

item.title: Loitering towards war
item.description: A history of how Vienna’s coffeehouses nurtured modernism while Belgrade’s kafanas incubated nationalist conspiracy and revolutionary violence.

item.title: The Dacre dynasty: how Britain’s rightwing press was radicalised
item.description: How Paul Dacre’s newsroom methods and protégés spread outrage-driven culture-war journalism across Britain’s nominally competing rightwing newspapers.

item.title: The Gulf has billions to spend on AI. It still needs Nvidia
item.description: Why Gulf states’ vast AI budgets cannot overcome Nvidia’s hardware lead, CUDA lock-in, and the geopolitical constraints attached to advanced chips.

item.title: Unmothered
item.description: A science-informed memoir about how parental estrangement can fracture autobiographical memory—and why our identities are partly co-authored by other people.

item.title: Why Am I Left-Handed?
item.description: An intimate biological mystery links fetal movement, spinal-cord asymmetry, microtubules, evolution, and humanity’s unusual bias toward the right hand.

item.title: China’s Open AI Models Are Advancing Its Global Soft Power
item.description: Why low-cost, adaptable open-weight models may turn AI distribution into geopolitical influence, ecosystem dependence, and supply-chain power.

item.title: A Truth-Teller for All 10 Villages
item.description: A Turkish comedian’s sweeping satire becomes a portrait of national hypocrisy—and a test of what authoritarian power cannot tolerate.

item.title: What Draws Well-Meaning Parents to Authoritarian Parenting?
item.description: How parental anxiety can disguise control as preparation—and why warm boundaries teach more than punishment or conditional approval.

item.title: Abyss
item.description: Ukrainian war widows’ testimonies challenge the autonomous self and the comforting idea that profound grief is simply something to overcome.

item.title: We Know Simple Fluids Can Flow. Turns Out, Some Can Fracture.
item.description: A nonelastic liquid was observed cracking like a brittle solid, challenging a basic assumption about how fluids fracture.

item.title: The Stretch of a Soul
item.description: An exploration of biography, philosophy and whether the fullness of a human life can exceed intellectual explanation.

item.title: B-Sides: Albert O. Hirschman’s “The Passions and the Interests”
item.description: Why capitalism was once defended as a way to tame destructive passions—and how prosperity can eventually threaten freedom.

item.title: ‘It Is Comforting to Be Haunted’: How Attitudes to Abortion Have Changed Through the Ages
item.description: A personal and historical essay restoring bodily experience and circumstance to abstract arguments about abortion.

item.title: Inside the Luddite Festival Harnessing Gen Z’s Rage Against Big Tech
item.description: A grassroots experiment in replacing platform dependence with offline events, analog tools and community-owned infrastructure.

item.title: Lore of the Rings
item.description: Valerie Trouet explains how ancient trees preserve precisely dated evidence of climate shifts, cosmic events and turning points in human history.

item.title: China’s AI Boom Is Creating a Different Kind of Entrepreneur
item.description: How Chinese workers are using AI, frugal innovation and one-person businesses to navigate weak growth, limited capital and precarious employment.

item.title: Seeds of War
item.description: An investigation of how war, foreign aid, import dependence and seed policy have deepened Lebanon’s long-running food crisis.

item.title: The Sumerian Roots of Zaha Hadid’s Architecture
item.description: Diana Darke traces Zaha Hadid’s celebrated futuristic forms to Iraqi landscapes, ancient Sumeria and the traditions of Islamic architecture.

item.title: ‘It Makes Your Heart Sing’: Can a Pioneering Project Show That Rewilding Really Works?
item.description: A reported examination of whether biodiversity credits, carbon markets and ecotourism can make large-scale ecological restoration financially viable.

item.title: Is Life Just Different?
item.description: Philip Ball asks whether biological agency can become a rigorous scientific idea without smuggling mysticism or design into biology.

item.title: The Fall and Rise of Screwworm
item.description: How sterile insects defeated a devastating parasite—and how maintenance failures allowed it to return.

item.title: China's Web Novel Platforms Embraced AI. Now They Are Fighting It
item.description: Chinese fiction platforms encouraged AI-assisted production, then discovered that abundance can destroy quality, trust, and reader value.

item.title: Who Do You Think You Are? There Are Two Answers
item.description: A useful distinction between the identity society recognizes and the deeper identity formed by your values, cares, and self-understanding.

item.title: How the World Celebrated America's 200th Birthday
item.description: America's 1976 bicentennial was not merely a domestic celebration but a global soft-power project shaped by foreign participation.

item.title: Skill nostalgia
item.description: The longing for manual skills reflects frustration with deskilled work and a desire for complexity, mastery, community, and meaning.

item.title: The People Who Will Thrive in the AI Age
item.description: In an AI-abundant world, the scarce human differentiator may be the willingness to exert mental effort rather than raw intelligence.

item.title: The Century of Eric Hobsbawm
item.description: An examination of a historian who made the 20th century intelligible for millions while remaining attached to communism.

item.title: What Makes Humans Stupid
item.description: Stupidity is not simply the absence of intelligence; consequential stupidity often requires intelligence organized badly.

item.title: The End of Reading Is Here
item.description: People still decode words, but the habits needed for sustained reading, synthesis, and independent thought are weakening.

item.title: America At 250 & Beyond
item.description: America’s next democratic evolution should combine citizen participation with deliberative institutions that resist populism and capture.

item.title: The Pulse: a new trend, smart model routing
item.description: Teams are routing each AI task to the cheapest model that can do it well enough instead of always using the strongest model.

item.title: Researchers Reveal the Power of ‘Quantum Proofs’
item.description: Some problems appear to require proofs that are themselves quantum objects, not ordinary classical descriptions.

item.title: When The Machines Deserve Our Consideration
item.description: We may never get definitive proof that AI systems are conscious, but we still need a practical standard for moral consideration.

item.title: How Amsterdam invented the fire department
item.description: Seventeenth-century Amsterdam reduced catastrophic fire damage through technology, organization, incentives, and civic capacity.

item.title: Semiquincentennial Blues
item.description: An essay on America’s 250th birthday, race, patriotic performance, national mythology, and political exhaustion.

item.title: The Meaning of Alienage for Wong Kim Ark
item.description: The stakes of birthright citizenship were daily rights, mobility, legal status, and the vulnerability of Chinese immigrants and their U.S.-born children.

item.title: For the First Time, a Cell Built From Scratch Grows and Divides
item.description: Synthetic biologists assembled nonliving components inside a membrane and produced a cell-like system that grew, copied DNA, and divided.

item.title: We Need A Way To Prove Personhood Online
item.description: AI agents are breaking the old internet assumption that most online actions trace back to an accountable person.

item.title: The war against ‘woke’ could end US science as we know it
item.description: A proposed OMB rule would give political appointees broad authority over research grants, collaborations, conferences, publishing costs, and alignment with presidential priorities.

item.title: Albanians Embrace Their ‘Flamingo Revolution’
item.description: Mass protests in Albania are challenging a luxury development project in the Vjosa-Narta Protected Area.

item.title: Searching for the Unexpected
item.description: A conversation with Carlo Ginzburg about microhistory, marginal voices, archives, chance, and intellectual surprise.
